11.text:
# 2025 Week 11 Recap

## The Unbeatens Finally Started Looking Vulnerable

The playoff race spent two months pretending hierarchy existed. Week 11 finally blew that illusion apart.

Three of the nation's top five teams lost Saturday, and none of the defeats felt fluky. **#1 Penn State** fell **31-30** at home to **#5 Indiana**, ending the Hoosiers' quiet climb from preseason afterthought to legitimate Big Ten threat. **#2 Georgia**, projected all season as the SEC's safest playoff anchor, walked into Starkville and left with a **36-29** loss to **#22 Mississippi State**, a team now sitting on three ranked wins. And **#3 Texas A&M**, carrying one of the cleanest résumés in the country with two quality wins already banked, escaped Columbia only because its offense found enough answers in a **41-35** shootout against **#11 Missouri**.

The common thread wasn't chaos for chaos' sake. It was pressure. The unbeaten and one-loss teams that spent September building margin for error are now discovering the schedule has teeth. Penn State's entire season had been defined by stability — the Nittany Lions had occupied the No. 1 spot every single week since the opening poll — but Indiana finally gave the Big Ten race a pulse. Georgia's stumble looked even worse because Mississippi State is no longer a cute surprise story; the Bulldogs are 8-2 with wins that now stack up against almost anyone in the country. And LSU, a year removed from finishing inside the playoff picture, survived Tuscaloosa with a **42-41** win over **#19 Alabama** that rescued a season drifting toward irrelevance.

The SEC entered the weekend with four teams projected into the CFP field. By Saturday night, the conference looked less dominant than deeply unstable.

## The Teams That Benefited Didn't Waste the Opening

That instability created oxygen elsewhere, and the programs just beneath the top tier took full advantage.

**#4 Miami** didn't overwhelm Syracuse in a **21-14** ACC win, but style points matter less in November than survival. The Hurricanes, sitting at 8-1 with a quality win already on the résumé, watched three teams ahead of them lose while they simply avoided disaster. **#6 Duke** did the same in a workmanlike **27-13** road result over Connecticut; the Blue Devils are still unbeaten, but the weekly question remains whether their résumé matches their record.

The teams with less ambiguity were lower in the rankings. **#7 Louisville** buried California **45-16**, another emphatic step for an ACC contender that has quietly spent the entire season inside the top 10. **#8 Notre Dame** survived a tricky matchup with **#24 Navy**, winning **31-24** in a game that kept the Irish firmly in the at-large conversation after opening the season ranked outside the top 10. And **#10 Texas Tech**, now 9-1 and carrying the Big 12's projected automatic bid, took care of BYU **24-16** while the teams above them cannibalized each other.

Then there's **Wyoming**, which may have the cleanest story in the country. The Cowboys moved to 9-0 by walking into Boise and winning **45-27**, preserving both their unbeaten season and their projected Group of Five auto-bid. Wyoming didn't begin the year ranked at all. Now the Mountain West's best team looks sturdier than half the power-conference playoff hopefuls ahead of it.

The committee can explain away one upset. It gets much harder when half the top five loses on the same day.

## The Poll Has Barely Changed All Season. That Ends Now.

One of the strangest features of this season has been how static the rankings stayed for two months. From Week 6 through Week 11, the top four never changed: Penn State, Georgia, Texas A&M, Miami. Week after week, the sport looked frozen.

Week 11 shattered that illusion.

Indiana spent the entire year hovering between fifth and ninth before finally cashing in the opportunity that comes with staying relevant long enough. Mississippi State followed a different trajectory — ranked only 22nd entering the weekend despite already owning three quality wins — and now has an argument as the SEC's most dangerous two-loss team. Meanwhile Clemson's slide has become impossible to ignore. The Tigers opened the year at No. 4 and never fully recovered from the perception that followed; after Saturday's **38-35** loss to Florida State, Clemson sits at 6-4 with only one quality win and a résumé nowhere near its preseason expectations.

At the opposite end, programs like Texas Tech and Wyoming have climbed by simply avoiding the collapses consuming everyone else. In a season that spent months pretending to have clear tiers, endurance is suddenly becoming a competitive advantage.

## Around the Country, November Turned Mean

The volatility wasn't limited to contenders.

Auburn, sitting at 4-6 with both a quality win and a bad loss already on the ledger, knocked off **#12 Vanderbilt 27-24**, another hit to an SEC middle class that keeps sabotaging itself. Iowa State strengthened its Big 12 standing by beating **#21 TCU 38-24**, while Florida State's upset of Clemson gave the Seminoles their second straight result capable of reshaping the ACC race.

Elsewhere, the Big Ten delivered some of the week's strangest contradictions. **Oregon** escaped Iowa **41-36** despite entering with a bad loss already hanging over its résumé, while Wisconsin — a 4-5 team with two quality wins — lit up Washington **46-35** in one of the conference's most confusing profiles. USC moved to 6-3 by dispatching Northwestern **38-17**, and UCLA stole a one-point game from Nebraska **28-27**.

Outside the power leagues, Toledo stayed unbeaten at 9-0 with a **27-14** win over Northern Illinois, Miami (OH) improved to 8-1 by beating Ohio, and James Madison dismantled Marshall **45-14** as the Sun Belt continued producing quietly dangerous teams beneath the national radar.

For most of the season, college football looked top-heavy. Week 11 suggested the opposite: the gap between contender and spoiler may be smaller than anyone wanted to admit.

12.text:
# 2025 Week 12 Recap

## The Middle of the Playoff Race Is Finally Breaking

For two months, college football's playoff picture has been crowded with good-not-great résumés pretending to be contenders. Week 12 finally started separating the teams that merely accumulated wins from the ones capable of surviving November.

That distinction mattered most in the SEC, where **#23 Texas' 31-28 road win over #18 Georgia** looked less like an upset and more like a résumé correction. The Longhorns entered the week at 6-4 with two quality wins but almost no margin for error left; now they're the rare four-loss team with multiple ranked victories and a late-season pulse. Georgia, meanwhile, spent most of the season coasting on the inertia of an 8-2 record and an early top-five reputation. The Bulldogs opened the year at #3 and stayed in the top two through Week 11 before the slide began. This loss turns that descent from wobble into trend.

Clemson's season lives in a similar gray area. The ACC contender escaped Louisville **20-17** to improve to 7-3, but the Tigers' profile still feels thinner than their ranking suggested a month ago. Clemson opened the year at #4 nationally and carried that number into Week 7 before the erosion started; surviving Louisville on the road keeps them alive, but not convincing. Louisville, sitting at 8-2 after beginning the season ranked #7 entering Week 5, has become the kind of dangerous second-tier team nobody wants to see in November.

The consequence of all this instability is simple: the sport's unbeaten and near-unbeaten teams are no longer sharing oxygen with the two-loss crowd. The separation line finally appeared Saturday.

## The Teams Above the Cut Line Took Care of Business

That divide became clearer because nearly every projected playoff team above the bubble handled Week 12 with varying degrees of authority.

**#1 Texas A&M** strengthened its at-large positioning by beating South Carolina **48-30**, continuing a climb that started outside the top two as recently as Week 10. The Aggies now sit 8-1 with two quality wins, and unlike several other contenders, they avoided the kind of November panic that has swallowed half the SEC. **#2 Duke**, now 10-0, kept its unbeaten season intact with a controlled **28-14** win over Virginia. Duke began the year outside the top 15 and has steadily risen while bigger brands around them collapsed.

The ACC's other heavyweight, **#3 Miami**, never let North Carolina State hang around for long in a **24-10** victory that pushed the Hurricanes to 9-1. Miami opened the season ranked #4, briefly disappeared from the top tier in September, then rebuilt itself into one of the country's steadiest playoff résumés. **#4 Penn State** did the same thing in the Big Ten, putting away Michigan State **42-21** after spending the first 11 weeks carrying the pressure of a preseason No. 1 ranking.

Elsewhere in the Big Ten, **#9 Oregon** dismantled Minnesota **41-7**, while **#8 Ohio State** moved to 8-2 by beating UCLA **38-20**. But Indiana's profile took a hit when Wisconsin walked into Bloomington and left with a **27-24** upset. The Hoosiers spent Weeks 5 through 11 living inside the top five before slipping to #10 entering this weekend, and this loss reinforces the concern around a résumé with only one quality win.

The SEC's safer contenders looked considerably stronger. **#6 LSU** buried Arkansas **35-10** to stay firmly inside the projected field, while **#11 Oklahoma** survived a defensive slog against Alabama **17-13**. Mississippi State kept stacking evidence with a **41-35** road win over Missouri; the Bulldogs now own three quality wins, more than any currently ranked SEC team in this field.

The takeaway from Week 12 is that the playoff race no longer looks overcrowded. The top dozen teams are starting to separate themselves from the teams merely borrowing ranking numbers.

## The Unbeatens Outside the Spotlight Are Becoming Impossible to Ignore

That same sorting process is elevating programs that spent most of the season outside the national conversation.

**#14 Wyoming** moved to 10-0 with a workmanlike **20-7** win at Fresno State, continuing one of the quietest unbeaten seasons in the country. The Cowboys entered Week 11 ranked just #17 before climbing into the projected Group of Five auto-bid slot, and every week they avoid chaos makes it harder to imagine them falling out. **#25 Toledo** finally lost, falling **38-16** to Miami (OH), but the Rockets' 10-0 start already changed the shape of the MAC race.

The American also keeps producing legitimate depth. **#17 Tulane** handled Florida Atlantic **38-10** to reach 8-2, while **#24 Navy** survived South Florida **31-28** in one of the week's better undercard games. East Carolina's **29-14** win over Memphis pushed the Pirates to 8-2 as well, giving the conference a cluster of teams that may not reach the playoff but absolutely can wreck somebody else's season.

Outside the rankings, a handful of results hinted at broader shifts. Northwestern's **35-21** win over Michigan dropped the Wolverines to 7-3 after they opened the season ranked as high as #6 entering Week 3. Kansas State embarrassed Oklahoma State **38-17** to keep its bowl hopes alive. Utah blasted Baylor **40-21**, and West Virginia's **42-21** win over Arizona State kept the Big 12's middle class volatile as ever.

Week 12 didn't produce a single earth-shattering upset. What it did produce was something more important in late November: clarity. The teams still standing now look much more like playoff teams than placeholders.

13.text:
# 2025 Week 13 Recap

## The Playoff Picture Didn't Break This Week — It Hardened

College football spent two months pretending chaos was inevitable. Week 13 pushed back. The teams that look like playoff teams kept surviving, and the teams with structural flaws finally stopped hiding them.

That doesn't mean Saturday lacked danger. **No. 4 Penn State needed a three-point escape against Nebraska. No. 5 Notre Dame got pushed deep into the fourth quarter by Syracuse. No. 7 Ohio State let Rutgers hang around into the final possession.** But the difference between November contenders and November cautionary tales is increasingly simple: the good teams are finding ways through imperfect Saturdays, while everyone else is leaking credibility.

Texas A&M never flirted with that line. The Aggies, projected as the No. 1 seed entering the week, handled business in a 45-0 dismissal of FCS Southeast and stayed atop a national landscape where attrition has become the defining trait. Duke, still unbeaten at 11-0, outlasted North Carolina 33-24 and continued one of the season's most dramatic rises after opening the year outside the top 10. Miami turned Virginia Tech into another résumé bullet point with a 38-7 road win that kept the Hurricanes squarely in the at-large conversation.

The sport spent September waiting for a superteam. November is revealing something different: a playoff race built on endurance.

## The SEC's Resume Problem Got Worse

That endurance test is especially visible in the SEC, where the league's middle class keeps shifting and one of its highest-ranked teams just absorbed the week's most damaging loss.

**LSU, ranked No. 6 entering the weekend and still carrying the glow of a playoff projection, lost 42-10 at home to Western Kentucky.** Not narrowly. Not on a last-minute turnover. The Tigers were buried by a 4-7 Conference USA team, the kind of loss that doesn't just dent a résumé — it changes the conversation around the entire profile.

The SEC still produced volume elsewhere. Oklahoma strengthened its at-large case with a 28-14 win over Missouri, Texas blanked Arkansas 29-0 for its second quality win of the season, and Vanderbilt moved to 8-3 by taking apart Kentucky 42-20. Tennessee, now 8-3 with two quality wins but one damaging loss on the sheet, overwhelmed Florida 45-14 and likely stabilized its position after a volatile month.

Georgia kept the lights on with a 47-7 rout of Charlotte, but the Bulldogs no longer look like the national standard they were entering the year. Back in Week 2, Georgia opened as the nation's No. 3 team and spent nearly half the season inside the top two. Now they're a two-loss team trying to hold position while others rise around them.

And while Texas A&M continues to look playoff-secure, the league beneath the Aggies feels unstable. LSU's collapse Saturday only reinforced that the SEC may have quantity, but not nearly as much certainty as it thought a month ago.

## The Big Ten's Top Tier Keeps Surviving the Margins

If the SEC spent Saturday absorbing résumé damage, the Big Ten spent it surviving close calls.

Penn State escaped Nebraska 27-24, avoiding what would have been the week's defining upset. Ohio State's 40-34 win over Rutgers was less comfortable than the Buckeyes likely wanted, especially for a program that opened the season ranked No. 2 nationally before spending much of the fall trying to climb back into the top tier. Oregon, meanwhile, may have delivered the conference's most important win: a 41-34 victory over USC that kept the Ducks firmly inside the projected at-large field.

Wisconsin also continued one of the season's strangest résumé builds. The Badgers moved to 6-5 by surviving Illinois 40-35, and their three quality wins now stand out far more than their overall record. That's the profile of a team capable of changing somebody else's postseason path in the final weeks.

Michigan avoided disaster against Maryland, Kansas escaped Iowa State 36-32 in the Big 12, and Colorado kept bowl positioning alive with a 28-20 win over Arizona State, a Sun Devils team that entered the season ranked as high as No. 23 before sliding back toward .500.

The Big Ten's elite still has playoff leverage. What it no longer has is room for another mistake.

## The Group of Five Race Is Down to Wyoming and Toledo

The most stable playoff candidacies in the country may belong outside the power structure.

**Wyoming moved to 11-0 by turning a halftime deficit into a 35-14 win over New Mexico, continuing a climb that began with the Cowboys ranked just No. 17 entering Week 11.** Jay Sawvel's team is now projected as the Group of Five automatic qualifier, and unlike many unbeaten outsiders in recent seasons, Wyoming has looked increasingly comfortable with the expectation.

Toledo is making the committee think harder than it expected to. The Rockets survived Ball State 27-20 to reach 11-0, preserving the MAC's cleanest résumé and extending one of the quietest unbeaten runs in the country.

Elsewhere in the Mountain West, Boise State improved to 7-4 with a 42-24 win over San Jose State, while UNLV reached 8-3 by outlasting Hawaii 38-32. Tulane stayed ranked with a 31-10 win over Temple, though the Green Wave now sit behind Wyoming in the projected Group of Five hierarchy.

The sport spent years debating whether an unbeaten Group of Five team could force its way into the playoff. In a 12-team format, the better question might be this: what happens if there are two?

## Around the Country, November Finally Started Separating Teams

That same separation line showed up everywhere else Saturday.

South Carolina entered the weekend 2-8 and still annihilated Coastal Carolina 48-6. Florida State moved to 7-4 with a 33-14 road win over NC State, a needed stabilizer for a team with one quality win and an uneven résumé. Pittsburgh quietly improved to 7-3 by beating Georgia Tech 37-24, while Northwestern reached 7-4 after a 37-18 win over Minnesota.

Some programs used Week 13 to re-enter relevance. UCF's 40-37 win over Oklahoma State pushed the Knights to 5-6 and kept bowl hopes alive. Houston did the same in a 22-21 escape against TCU. Stanford's 42-31 win over California gave the Cardinal a fourth victory after spending most of the season buried near the bottom of the ACC standings.

And then there was Old Dominion, now 10-1 after Georgia Southern's latest loss. In a season dominated by playoff math and ranked-team anxiety, the Monarchs have quietly built one of the best records in the country outside the spotlight.

That's the truth about Week 13. The playoff race narrowed, but college football itself didn't. November always reveals new contenders. It also reveals who was never really one to begin with.

14.text:
# 2025 Week 14 Recap

## Rivalry Week Didn’t Clarify the Playoff Race — It Exposed How Thin the Margin Really Is

For two months, the playoff picture looked like it belonged to a handful of heavyweights. Week 14 argued the opposite: almost every contender spent rivalry weekend proving how vulnerable it really is.

Texas A&M stayed at the top of the sport despite losing **35-29** to Texas, because the Aggies had already built the strongest résumé in the country with two quality wins and a 10-1 mark entering the weekend. But rivalry week still stripped away the illusion that any contender is untouchable. Ohio State buried Michigan **38-10** after entering the year ranked No. 2 and wobbling through midseason turbulence. Oklahoma, sitting outside the top five for much of the year despite a 10-win profile, turned LSU into a non-factor in a **27-7** result that looked far more authoritative than the Sooners’ ranking history suggested. And Miami kept rolling with a **45-27** win at Pittsburgh, strengthening the case that the Hurricanes — not the preseason favorites around them — have become the ACC’s steadiest force.

Elsewhere, rivalry games kept turning playoff résumés into contradictions. Clemson improved to 9-3 by dismantling South Carolina **35-7**, but the Tigers are still carrying a bad loss that continues to separate them from the sport’s cleanest résumés. Georgia closed 10-2 after a **42-23** win over Georgia Tech, though the Bulldogs spent much of November sliding from the No. 2 spot they held through midseason. Louisville, another team that climbed steadily while bigger brands stumbled, moved to 10-2 with a **37-25** road win over Kentucky.

The lesson from this weekend is uncomfortable for the committee: nearly every contender has looked elite for stretches, and nearly every contender has spent another stretch looking entirely ordinary.

## The Sport’s Most Stable Teams Suddenly Aren’t the Ones Anyone Expected

That instability is why Duke’s season now looks less like a cute story and more like a structural threat to the playoff hierarchy.

The Blue Devils finished 12-0 after a controlled **30-15** win over Wake Forest, extending the most consistent season in the country outside Wyoming’s unbeaten run. Duke opened the year outside the top 10, climbed methodically through the fall, and now sits No. 2 nationally entering Week 15. Miami followed a similar trajectory. The Hurricanes entered Week 5 ranked fourth, disappeared briefly from the very top tier of the conversation, and quietly built an 11-1 résumé that now has them sitting No. 3.

Even programs with more visible flaws benefited from the chaos around them. Indiana rebounded from late-season turbulence by routing Purdue **45-6** to finish 10-2. Notre Dame escaped Stanford **35-27** to maintain its at-large position. Oregon survived a dangerous Washington team **35-28**, but the Ducks’ inability to fully separate from a 5-6 rival reinforced the same question that has hovered over much of the top 10: how many teams actually look dominant right now?

Penn State might be the clearest example of the sport’s volatility. The Nittany Lions opened the season ranked No. 1, spent weeks occupying the top line of the poll, and now enter Week 15 sitting 13th after losing **32-31** to Rutgers. No contender has fallen farther without completely collapsing out of relevance.

The sport spent September searching for superteams. By December, it has mostly found résumés with dents.

## The Middle Tier of the Rankings Became the Week’s Real Story

While the playoff field sorted itself out at the top, the teams around the edges of the rankings produced the weekend’s most revealing results.

West Virginia, now 7-5 and ranked 23rd, stole a huge Big 12 result by surviving Texas Tech **38-35**. The Mountaineers still carry a bad loss, but they also closed the year playing their best football. Brigham Young kept its ranking alive with a **35-29** win over UCF, while Kansas State pushed Colorado to 6-6 with a **31-20** road result that underscored how unforgiving the Big 12 became outside the playoff picture.

The American produced the same kind of depth chaos. Tulane avoided a damaging loss by escaping Charlotte **31-27**, while North Texas strengthened its late push into the rankings with a **38-16** win over Temple. East Carolina lost **50-48** to Florida Atlantic and still remained ranked because the Pirates entered the week with a 10-1 résumé that had already carried significant weight. Old Dominion, meanwhile, kept one of the quietest 11-win seasons in the country alive with a **45-38** win over Georgia State.

And then there’s Wyoming. The Cowboys moved to 12-0 by surviving Hawaii **35-28**, finishing a regular season that started with almost no national expectations and ended with the Mountain West’s only unbeaten team carrying legitimate playoff pressure into championship week.

Week 14 didn’t produce a single seismic upset that reordered the sport. It produced something more unsettling: evidence that college football’s entire top tier is balancing on margins that keep shrinking.

15.text:
# 2025 Week 15 Recap

The regular season ended with less chaos than the sport spent three months promising — which may be the strangest result of all.

For weeks, the national picture had behaved like a centrifuge. Texas A&M climbed to No. 1 by Week 12 and disappeared from the Week 15 snapshot entirely. Duke opened 12-0 yet entered Week 15 outside the top 15. Florida reached the edge of the CFP projection despite a 6-6 record. Tennessee spent half the season hovering around the Top 25 before falling out altogether. The season's defining trait has not been dominance; it has been volatility.

And then Week 15 arrived with the one game college football never needs help selling.

## Army-Navy Closed the Regular Season the Way the American Race Actually Looked

**Navy 21, Army 10** was not a playoff eliminator or a top-10 collision, but it fit the shape of this season better than most ranked games would have.

Navy finished 9-3 and avoided the late fade that swallowed several American contenders across the country. Army, meanwhile, closed 4-8 with four bad losses on its résumé, a season that never stabilized after an uneven start. In a year when Tulane entered Week 15 ranked No. 16 and projected for the Group of Five autobid, the conference still spent most of the fall sorting through tiers rather than establishing a clear pecking order.

That matters because the American produced two very different kinds of success stories simultaneously. Tulane stayed nationally relevant at 10-2, North Texas and East Carolina each reached 10-2 outside the rankings, and Navy quietly built a nine-win season that looked sturdier by December than it did in October. Army's loss Saturday also underscored the other side of the league: the middle collapsed fast. Four American teams finished with at least eight losses.

The sport spent much of November obsessing over the SEC and Big Ten playoff math, but the American may have been deeper top-to-bottom than its national reputation suggested.

## The Poll Never Settled — It Just Ran Out of Weeks

That instability shows up everywhere in the rankings history.

Penn State began the season at No. 1, disappeared from the top spot by Week 10, then returned to the top entering Week 15 at 10-2. Ohio State opened No. 2 in Week 2, cratered to No. 15 by Week 3, and still climbed back to No. 2 entering championship season. Georgia followed the opposite arc: preseason certainty, midseason slippage, then a recovery to No. 3 despite owning only one quality win.

No trajectory was stranger than Duke's. The Blue Devils climbed as high as No. 2 entering Week 13 while still unbeaten, yet entered Week 15 at No. 18 despite a 12-0 record and a projected conference championship path. Clemson, meanwhile, stayed present almost the entire season and enters the postseason 9-3 with two quality wins and one bad loss — the résumé of a dangerous team, but not a clean one.

The SEC's shape may be even harder to explain. Texas A&M spent multiple weeks atop the rankings and still sits only No. 12 in the current CFP projection despite an 11-1 record and two quality wins. Florida is projected into the field at 6-6. Texas finished 7-5 but owns two quality wins, the same total as Clemson and Tennessee. Ole Miss enters bowl season ranked at 8-4 despite two bad losses.

This was not a season where records clarified anything. Résumés overpowered aesthetics almost every week.

## The Conferences That Quietly Won the Year

While the SEC consumed most of the attention, the Big Ten and ACC built the broader national depth.

The Big Ten placed Penn State, Ohio State, Indiana, Oregon, Michigan, Wisconsin and USC in the Week 15 rankings. Indiana's rise may be the most sustainable story in the sport: the Hoosiers opened the year already ranked, stayed in the national conversation wire-to-wire, and now sit inside the projected top eight at 10-2 as a projected conference champion. Oregon survived an uneven middle stretch and still reached 10-2. Michigan absorbed enough losses to fall from the early top six but still finished ranked at 9-4.

The ACC answered with a different model. Miami spent nearly the entire season inside the top four and enters the postseason 11-1. Duke went undefeated. Louisville reached 10-2. Clemson stayed relevant all year despite turbulence. Even Wake Forest and Pittsburgh finished 8-4. The conference lacked one overwhelming juggernaut, but it may have had the healthiest middle class in the country.

Outside the power structure, Old Dominion's 11-1 season in the Sun Belt and Miami (OH)'s 10-2 run in the MAC gave the rankings texture beyond the usual brands. Toledo reached 11-1 without entering Week 15 ranked. Wyoming finished 12-0 entirely outside the Top 25 snapshot.

That's the lasting argument of the 2025 regular season: college football kept waiting for the hierarchy to restore itself, and it never really did.

# 2026 Season Preview

## The Poll Starts With Oregon, but the Gap Is Thin

The top of 2026 feels less settled than the rankings suggest. **Oregon** opens at No. 1 after finishing No. 1 in the 2025 final poll, but the teams directly behind the Ducks all carry arguments that this race could swing quickly once league play begins.

**Texas A&M**, **Oklahoma**, **Georgia**, **Notre Dame**, and **Ohio State** round out the top six, and each enters the year after finishing inside the final top 12 last season. That matters because last year's poll did not produce a runaway national hierarchy beyond Oregon. **Ohio State** finished No. 2 in 2025 and starts sixth now, while **Georgia** begins fourth after ending last season at No. 12, a sign that expectations shifted dramatically over the offseason even without a new game played.

The SEC and Big Ten dominate the upper half of the rankings. **Texas A&M**, **Oklahoma**, **Georgia**, **Tennessee**, **Ole Miss**, **Mississippi State**, and **Alabama** all begin ranked out of the SEC, while the Big Ten counters with **Oregon**, **Ohio State**, **Penn State**, **Indiana**, **Wisconsin**, **USC**, **Michigan**, and **Northwestern**. There is depth everywhere.

## Last Season Changed the Shape of the Sport

Oregon's national title push now becomes the standard everyone else is chasing. The Ducks finished No. 1, but the 2025 season also produced a crowded second tier that now fills much of the preseason top 25.

Several programs translated breakthrough years directly into major preseason expectations. **Duke** finished sixth and opens 2026 at No. 13. **Louisville** climbed from a No. 9 final ranking to No. 12 in the preseason. **Indiana** went from No. 10 to No. 14 and now enters a loaded Big Ten race where even a strong record may not guarantee playoff positioning.

Some teams enter with pressure moving the other direction. **Miami** finished fourth and opens eighth. **Penn State** slipped from eighth to tenth. **Clemson** landed seventh after ending 2025 at No. 13. Whether those placements reflect skepticism or simply the depth of the field is one of the harder questions entering September.

Outside the power conferences, last season's final poll showed unusual national reach. **Tulane**, **East Carolina**, and **North Texas** all finished ranked from the American, while **Wyoming** closed at No. 11 out of the Mountain West and **James Madison**, **Old Dominion**, and **Lafayette** gave the Sun Belt three ranked finishers. Only Tulane returns to the preseason top 25, opening at No. 21, which leaves plenty of room for another surprise climb from outside the traditional contenders.

## Sustained Winners Are Starting to Separate

A few programs already look entrenched near the top of this dynasty's early era. Oregon followed a national No. 1 finish with another preseason No. 1 ranking. **Texas A&M**, **Ohio State**, **Notre Dame**, **Oklahoma**, and **Miami** all finished in the 2025 top seven and return inside the preseason top eight. That kind of carryover matters in a landscape this crowded.

The ACC may be the deepest conference week to week. **Clemson**, **Miami**, **Louisville**, **Duke**, and **SMU** all enter ranked, and four of those teams finished inside last season's final top 13. There is very little separation between them entering the year.

The Big Ten offers a different problem. Oregon sits at the top, but **Ohio State**, **Penn State**, **Indiana**, **Wisconsin**, **USC**, **Michigan**, and **Northwestern** all begin ranked as well. One loss in that league may not eliminate a contender. Two probably changes everything.

## The Heisman Standard Already Exists

**John Mateer** swept the 2025 Heisman, Maxwell, and Davey O'Brien awards, setting an early benchmark for the sport heading into 2026. That sweep gives this season a clear individual standard even without additional returning-player information available.

What comes next is less certain. Oregon owns the No. 1 ranking, the SEC has overwhelming top-25 volume, and the ACC and Big Ten both look deep enough to produce multiple playoff-caliber teams. The season's central tension is simple: whether one program can separate from the field, or whether 2026 becomes a weekly reshuffling among a dozen legitimate contenders.

# 2026 Week 1 Recap

No. 6 **Ohio State** opened the 2026 season with the week's biggest result, beating No. 2 **Texas A&M** 38-28 on the road Saturday night. The Buckeyes, who finished No. 2 nationally last season after falling in the national championship game, moved to No. 1 in the Week 2 poll, while Texas A&M slid to No. 11 after opening 0-1.

The opening weekend also included a top-10 win for **Tennessee**, a road victory for **Georgia** against another ranked opponent, and an 80-point performance from defending national champion **Oregon**.

## Top games

### Ohio State 38, Texas A&M 28

Ohio State went into College Station and left with the top spot in the Week 2 rankings after beating preseason No. 2 Texas A&M 38-28. The Buckeyes rose from No. 6 to No. 1 in the new poll, while the Aggies — who finished No. 3 in 2025 and reached the CFP quarterfinals — fell to No. 11.

### Georgia 45, Duke 20

No. 4 Georgia opened with a road win over No. 13 Duke, pulling away for a 45-20 result in Durham. Duke entered the season after a No. 6 finish and a CFP quarterfinal appearance in 2025, but dropped to No. 23 in the Week 2 rankings after the loss. Georgia, which finished No. 12 last season, remained unbeaten and added an early ranked win.

### Tennessee 41, Louisville 31

No. 9 Tennessee moved into the top 10 of the Week 2 poll after beating No. 12 Louisville 41-31. The Volunteers climbed to No. 8 after opening 1-0, while Louisville — a CFP first-round team a season ago — fell to No. 20.

### SMU 45, Mississippi State 42

No. 17 SMU picked up one of the week's tighter ranked wins, outscoring No. 15 Mississippi State 45-42 in Dallas. The Mustangs moved up four spots to No. 13, while Mississippi State dropped to No. 21 after the loss.

## Around the Top 25

Defending national champion **Oregon** opened with an 80-0 victory over FCS Northwest and remained No. 2 in the Week 2 poll. No. 3 **Oklahoma**, a CFP semifinalist last season, scored 56 points against FCS Northwest and stayed at No. 3.

No. 5 **Notre Dame** beat Coastal Carolina 27-10 to begin another season in the top five after last year's CFP semifinal run. No. 7 **Clemson** beat FCS Midwest 52-17 and held at No. 6, while No. 8 **Miami** opened with a 49-0 win over FCS Northwest and moved to No. 7 after last year's No. 4 finish.

No. 10 **Penn State** beat UNLV 44-14, and No. 11 **Ole Miss** defeated Wake Forest 31-17. **Indiana**, which finished No. 10 nationally last season and reached the CFP quarterfinals, beat Connecticut 49-21 and climbed to No. 12.

No. 16 **Texas Tech** opened with a 52-7 victory over FCS East and moved to No. 14, while No. 18 **Wisconsin** beat Colorado State 31-8 to rise three spots. **USC** and **Michigan** each scored at least 56 points in wins over FCS opponents, and **Tulane** opened with a 45-20 win against Jacksonville State.

No. 22 **Alabama** scored 66 points against FCS West, and No. 23 **Kansas State** beat FCS East 49-7. No. 25 **Northwestern** survived a late challenge from San Jose State in a 35-31 win.

The week's ranked upset came in Boise, where **Boise State** beat No. 24 **Brigham Young** 38-21. BYU opened the season ranked after a No. 25 finish and Independence Bowl win in 2025 but fell out of the Week 2 rankings with the loss.

## Poll movement

Ohio State made the week's biggest jump, moving from No. 6 to No. 1 after beating Texas A&M. Oregon slipped from No. 1 to No. 2 despite the 80-point opener, while Tennessee climbed from No. 9 to No. 8 after its win over Louisville.

SMU moved from No. 17 to No. 13 after beating Mississippi State. Wisconsin climbed from No. 18 to No. 15, and Indiana rose two spots to No. 12.

Texas A&M fell from No. 2 to No. 11 after the loss to Ohio State. Louisville dropped eight spots to No. 20, Mississippi State fell from No. 15 to No. 21, and Duke slid from No. 13 to No. 23. BYU dropped out of the rankings entirely after losing at Boise State.

## Around the country

Illinois opened with one of the stronger road performances of the weekend, beating Iowa State 52-31 in Ames. Minnesota also picked up a Big Ten-over-Big 12 road win, defeating TCU 38-24.

Arizona State beat Florida State 31-24 in Tempe, and UCF opened with a 21-13 win over Iowa. Purdue held Oklahoma State to seven points in a 27-7 victory in West Lafayette.

Kent State produced one of the week's surprises with a 59-57 road win over West Virginia. Bowling Green also won on the road, beating Old Dominion 48-41.

Wyoming, which finished No. 11 in the final 2025 poll, opened with a 55-35 victory over Charlotte. LSU posted the week's highest total among FBS-vs.-FBS teams, scoring 72 points in a win over FCS Southeast.

Washington State nearly opened with a home upset before Texas escaped Pullman with a 31-28 victory. Arkansas beat North Carolina State 31-26, and Auburn avoided an opening loss by beating Louisiana Tech 24-20 on the road.

The Week 2 poll now features four Big Ten teams in the top 10 — Ohio State, Oregon, Penn State and Tennessee opponent Indiana just outside at No. 12 — while the SEC placed Oklahoma, Tennessee, Ole Miss, Texas A&M and Alabama in the top 18.

2.text:
# 2026 Week 2 Recap

## Top games

No. 15 **Wisconsin** moved into the top half of the Week 3 poll after a **55-7** road win over No. 14 **Texas Tech** on Saturday. The Badgers, who finished No. 22 in 2025, jumped from No. 15 to No. 13 in the Week 3 rankings, while Texas Tech fell seven spots to No. 21 after dropping to 1-1.

**Wyoming** stayed unbeaten with a **31-24** win over No. 22 **Kansas State** in Laramie, giving the Mountain West program its first ranked win of the season. Wyoming entered the year after an 11th-place finish in the 2025 final poll and remained ranked at No. 25 entering Week 3, while Kansas State fell out of the rankings after the loss.

No. 18 **Alabama** pulled away late for a **28-23** road win at No. 19 **Tulane** in one of the weekend's closest ranked matchups. Alabama improved to 2-0 and climbed three spots to No. 15 in the Week 3 rankings. Tulane, which finished No. 17 nationally last season, slipped four spots to No. 23.

The defending national champion **Oregon Ducks** stayed at No. 2 in the Week 3 poll after a **49-21** win at Kansas. The Ducks, who opened the season ranked No. 1 before Ohio State moved ahead of them entering Week 2, improved to 2-0 behind the road victory over the Big 12 program.

**Washington** delivered one of the week's most decisive ranked wins with a **34-7** result at No. 16 **USC**. The Huskies improved to 2-0 and strengthened their early résumé with a second quality win, though they remained outside the Week 3 Top 25 despite holding the projected Big Ten automatic bid in the current CFP projection.

Arkansas handed No. 12 **Indiana** its first loss, beating the Hoosiers **20-17** in Bloomington. Indiana, which reached the CFP quarterfinals and finished No. 10 nationally last season, dropped to No. 24 in the Week 3 rankings.

## Around the Top 25

No. 1 **Ohio State**, the national runner-up a season ago, improved to 2-0 with a **34-16** win over Appalachian State and held the top spot entering Week 3. No. 3 **Oklahoma** opened SEC play at 2-0 after a **58-3** victory over Hawaii, while No. 4 **Georgia** beat FCS West **55-13**.

No. 5 **Notre Dame** scored 56 points for the second straight week in a **56-35** win over North Texas. The Irish reached the CFP semifinals last season and remained fifth in the Week 3 rankings. No. 6 **Clemson**, which reached the CFP last year, beat Georgia Southern **56-21** to stay unbeaten.

No. 8 **Tennessee** moved to 2-0 with a **38-24** win over Virginia Tech. No. 9 **Penn State** posted the week's largest shutout among ranked teams, beating FCS Northwest **62-0**, and No. 10 **Ole Miss** beat FCS East **49-3**.

No. 11 **Texas A&M** rebounded from its opening-week loss with a **59-10** win over FCS Southeast. No. 13 **SMU** improved to 2-0 after a **45-24** victory over FCS West, and No. 17 **Michigan** won **45-28** at Cincinnati before rising to No. 14 in the Week 3 poll.

No. 20 **Louisville** scored the most points by any ranked team this week in a **69-6** win over FCS Northwest. No. 21 **Mississippi State** answered its Week 1 loss with a **59-20** victory at Nevada, while No. 24 **Northwestern** survived a road game at Northern Illinois, winning **35-31**.

## Poll movement

Ohio State remained No. 1 for the second straight week after beginning the preseason at No. 6. Oregon stayed at No. 2 despite opening the year atop the rankings, while Oklahoma and Georgia held steady in the next two spots entering Week 3.

Wisconsin produced the week's largest rise among ranked teams, moving from No. 15 to No. 13 after the win at Texas Tech. Alabama climbed from No. 18 to No. 15 following its road victory over Tulane, and Michigan moved from No. 17 to No. 14 after beating Cincinnati.

Texas Tech fell from No. 14 to No. 21 after the loss to Wisconsin. Tulane slid four spots to No. 23, and Indiana dropped from No. 12 to No. 24 after losing to Arkansas. USC, Kansas State and preseason No. 23 Duke all fell out of the rankings entering Week 3.

Houston entered the rankings at No. 22 after improving to 2-0 with a **21-17** win over South Florida, while Utah debuted at No. 25 following a **51-24** win over North Carolina State.

## Around the country

**Memphis** picked up one of the week's notable road wins with a **31-28** victory at Texas. The American program improved to 2-1, while the Longhorns dropped to 1-1.

**Nebraska** opened 2-0 after a **52-14** win at West Virginia, and **UCLA** scored 57 points in a **57-15** victory over UTSA. **LSU** also crossed the 60-point mark, beating Charlotte **62-17** in Baton Rouge.

**Iowa State** won the Cy-Hawk matchup with a **23-15** road victory over Iowa. The Cyclones entered the season after a Liberty Bowl win, while Iowa fell to 0-2 despite coming off a bowl victory last year.

**Utah** improved to 2-0 with a **51-24** win over North Carolina State and moved into the Week 3 rankings at No. 25. **Arizona State** also stayed unbeaten after a **38-21** road win at Vanderbilt, and **Colorado** improved to 2-0 with a **24-13** win over Syracuse.

Conference USA's **New Mexico State** moved to 3-0 with wins over Tulsa and Kennesaw State during the week. The Aggies outscored those opponents 81-34 and are one of the few unbeaten teams outside the Power conferences entering Week 3.

Week 3 opens with Ohio State, Oregon, Oklahoma, Georgia and Notre Dame holding the top five spots in the rankings, while eight SEC teams and eight Big Ten teams remain in the Top 25.

3.text:
# 2026 Week 3 Recap

## Georgia beats Oklahoma 40-37; Oregon, Ole Miss take losses

Georgia won the week's biggest game Saturday, beating **#3 Oklahoma 40-37** in Norman in a matchup between top-four SEC teams. The Bulldogs entered the week ranked fourth and left it as the new **No. 1 team in the Week 4 poll**, improving to 3-0 while handing Oklahoma its first loss of the season. A year after finishing No. 12 and winning the Gasparilla Bowl, Georgia now sits atop the rankings through three weeks.

The other major result came in the Big Ten, where defending national champion Oregon stayed unbeaten but was pushed by Oklahoma State before escaping with a **42-31** road win. The Ducks moved from No. 2 to No. 3 in the Week 4 rankings and remain 3-0.

Week 3 also produced one of the season's biggest upsets when **Boise State defeated #10 Ole Miss 31-28** in Idaho. The Mountain West program improved to 3-0 and added another ranked win to its résumé, while Ole Miss fell from the top 10 to No. 21 in the new poll.

Several ranked teams faced road tests against conference opponents. **West Virginia beat #25 Utah 42-36** in a Big 12 matchup, while **Wyoming routed Arkansas 44-6** to move to 3-0. The Cowboys entered the season after an 11th-place finish in the 2025 final poll and remained unbeaten with their third victory.

## Around the Top 25

The rest of the ranked field largely took care of business. **#11 Texas A&M** defeated Kentucky 24-13, **#8 Tennessee** won 44-31 at Auburn, and **#7 Miami** improved to 2-0 with a 55-16 victory over South Florida. Miami finished No. 4 nationally last season and remains unbeaten.

**#6 Clemson** posted one of the week's highest point totals in a 63-21 win over San Jose State. The Tigers, who reached the CFP last season, are 3-0 and climbed to No. 5 in the Week 4 rankings. **#5 Notre Dame**, a 2025 CFP semifinalist, improved to 3-0 with a 45-7 win over FCS Southeast and moved up one spot to No. 4.

In the Big Ten, **#9 Penn State** beat Ball State 41-11, **#14 Michigan** shut out UTEP 38-0, **#18 Northwestern** defeated Rutgers 49-21, and **#13 Wisconsin** won 45-17 over FCS Northwest. **#24 Indiana** opened with a 41-3 victory over FCS East.

The SEC also saw strong performances from **#15 Alabama**, which defeated Rice 66-6, and **#17 Mississippi State**, which beat FCS West 52-14. **#21 Texas Tech** answered a trip to UNLV with a 35-7 victory, while **#20 Duke** blanked FCS East 48-0 and Louisville won 41-3 at Western Michigan.

## Poll movement

Georgia's win over Oklahoma produced the biggest change at the top of the rankings. The Bulldogs rose from No. 4 entering Week 3 to **No. 1 entering Week 4**, while Oklahoma moved from No. 3 to No. 8.

Oregon remained unbeaten and moved from No. 2 to No. 3. Notre Dame climbed from No. 5 to No. 4, Clemson advanced from No. 6 to No. 5, and Miami moved from No. 7 to No. 6.

The largest drop among ranked teams belonged to Ole Miss, which fell from No. 10 to No. 21 after the loss at Boise State. Utah dropped out of the rankings following its defeat against West Virginia. LSU entered the poll at No. 25 after improving to 3-0.

## Around the country

Several unranked teams posted notable results outside the rankings race. **LSU defeated Missouri 48-7** in an SEC matchup and entered the Week 4 poll. **Florida shut out Pittsburgh 34-0**, while **Washington** improved to 3-0 with a 38-13 win over Washington State.

In the Big 12, **BYU defeated South Alabama 61-7**, **Baylor beat Arizona State 21-13**, **Kansas won 27-10 at California**, and **Cincinnati earned a 34-17 road victory over Appalachian State**.

The Mountain West continued a strong weekend beyond Boise State's upset. **Fresno State improved to 3-0** with a 21-15 win over UTSA, **Colorado State beat Western Kentucky 35-28**, **Nevada defeated Air Force 21-13**, and **New Mexico won 31-10 over Ohio**.

Other notable results included **USC's 47-17 victory over NC State**, **Minnesota's 38-28 win over UCLA**, **Maryland's 31-16 win over San Diego State**, **Wake Forest's 31-20 road win at Kansas State**, and **Eastern Michigan's 15-14 upset of Oklahoma State**.

Week 4 opens with Georgia at No. 1, Oregon at No. 3, Notre Dame at No. 4, Clemson at No. 5, Miami at No. 6 and Tennessee at No. 7 in the latest rankings.
